summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the mean of the data set {2, 4, 6, 8, 10}?

The mean is calculated by adding all values and dividing by the count: (2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10) / 5 = 30 / 5 = 6.

What is the mode of {5, 5, 8, 10, 12}?

The mode is the value that appears most frequently. Since 5 appears twice and all other values appear once, the mode is 5.

What is the mean of {10, 20, 30}?

The mean is (10 + 20 + 30) / 3 = 60 / 3 = 20.

If the data set {1, 3, 5, 7, x} has a mean of 5, what is x?

Mean = sum / count, so 5 = (1 + 3 + 5 + 7 + x) / 5. Solving: 25 = 16 + x, so x = 9.

What is the mean of a data set?

The mean is the arithmetic average, calculated by adding all values in the data set and dividing by the total number of values.

What is the median?

The median is the middle value when all data points are sorted from least to greatest. For an even number of values, it is the average of the two middle values.

What is the mode?

The mode is the value (or values) that appear most often in a data set. A data set can have no mode, one mode, or multiple modes.

What is an outlier?

An outlier is an extreme value that falls far from the rest of the data and can significantly affect the mean while having little effect on the median.

What is a weighted mean?

A weighted mean assigns different importance (weights) to data values, computed as the sum of (value times weight) divided by the sum of all weights.

What is a 'resistant' measure of center?

A resistant (or robust) measure is one that does not change much when outliers are present. The median is resistant, while the mean is not.

What is the 'frequency' of a data value?

Frequency is the count of how many times a particular value occurs in a data set. It is used to determine the mode.

A teacher records the following test scores for 5 students: 72, 85, 90, 68, and 95. What is the mean test score for the class?

The mean is (72 + 85 + 90 + 68 + 95) / 5 = 410 / 5 = 82. This represents the average performance of the class.

A bakery sells the following numbers of cupcakes each day over 6 days: 30, 45, 30, 50, 30, 55. What is the mode, and what does it tell the baker?

The mode is 30 because it appears three times, more than any other value. This tells the baker that 30 cupcakes is the most frequently occurring daily sales figure.

A gym owner records the ages of members in an evening class: 22, 25, 27, 30, 31, 33, 35. What is the median age of the class?

The data is already in order with 7 values, so the median is the 4th value: 30. The gym owner can use this to understand that the typical member in the class is about 30 years old.

A soccer coach tracks the number of goals scored per game over 8 games: 1, 0, 3, 2, 1, 1, 4, 0. What is the mean number of goals per game?

The mean is (1 + 0 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 0) / 8 = 12 / 8 = 1.5 goals per game.

A student earns the following scores on four exams weighted differently: Exam 1 (weight 10%) = 80, Exam 2 (weight 20%) = 90, Exam 3 (weight 30%) = 70, Exam 4 (weight 40%) = 85. What is the weighted mean?

Weighted mean = (80 x 0.10) + (90 x 0.20) + (70 x 0.30) + (85 x 0.40) = 8 + 18 + 21 + 34 = 81.0.

summary_sections.title: Calculation Methods
summary_sections.content_html:
How to Calculate

Example: What is the mean of the data set {2, 4, 6, 8, 10}?

Solution: The mean is calculated by adding all values and dividing by the count: (2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10) / 5 = 30 / 5 = 6.

Example: Find the median of {3, 7, 9, 12, 15}.

Solution: To find the median, sort the values and pick the middle one. The sorted data has 5 values, so the middle value at position 3 is 9.

Example: What is the mean of {10, 20, 30}?

Solution: The mean is (10 + 20 + 30) / 3 = 60 / 3 = 20.

Example: Find the median of {4, 8, 12, 16}.

Solution: With an even number of data points, the median is the average of the two middle values: (8 + 12) / 2 = 10.

Example: Find the mean of {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}.

Solution: The mean is (10 + 20 + 30 + 40 + 50) / 5 = 150 / 5 = 30.

Example: If the data set {1, 3, 5, 7, x} has a mean of 5, what is x?

Solution: Mean = sum / count, so 5 = (1 + 3 + 5 + 7 + x) / 5. Solving: 25 = 16 + x, so x = 9.

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

The mean uses every value in its calculation, so extreme values (outliers) pull the mean toward them, while the median and mode remain relatively unaffected.

An outlier is an extreme value that falls far from the rest of the data and can significantly affect the mean while having little effect on the median.

A resistant (or robust) measure is one that does not change much when outliers are present. The median is resistant, while the mean is not.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A teacher records the following test scores for 5 students: 72, 85, 90, 68, and 95. What is the mean test score for the class?

The mean is (72 + 85 + 90 + 68 + 95) / 5 = 410 / 5 = 82. This represents the average performance of the class.

Example: A store owner tracks daily sales (in dollars) for one week: $120, $150, $130, $800, $140, $135, $125. Which measure of center best represents the typical daily sales?

The $800 is an outlier that would pull the mean much higher than the typical values. The median ($135) better represents a typical day's sales because it is resistant to outliers.

Example: A bakery sells the following numbers of cupcakes each day over 6 days: 30, 45, 30, 50, 30, 55. What is the mode, and what does it tell the baker?

The mode is 30 because it appears three times, more than any other value. This tells the baker that 30 cupcakes is the most frequently occurring daily sales figure.

Example: A student's grades on six math quizzes are 78, 82, 90, 85, 88, and 92. If the student wants to achieve a mean of 88 after a seventh quiz, what score must the student earn?

Current sum = 78 + 82 + 90 + 85 + 88 + 92 = 515. For a mean of 88 over 7 quizzes, the total must be 88 x 7 = 616. So the seventh quiz must be 616 - 515 = 101.

Example: A group of friends compares how many hours they sleep each night: 6, 7, 7, 8, 8, 8, 9. A friend who sleeps 14 hours due to illness joins the group. How does this affect the mean compared to the median?

The 14-hour value is an outlier that raises the mean from about 7.6 to about 8.4, while the median only shifts from 8 to 8, showing that the median is resistant to outliers.

Example: A gym owner records the ages of members in an evening class: 22, 25, 27, 30, 31, 33, 35. What is the median age of the class?

The data is already in order with 7 values, so the median is the 4th value: 30. The gym owner can use this to understand that the typical member in the class is about 30 years old.

Example: A soccer coach tracks the number of goals scored per game over 8 games: 1, 0, 3, 2, 1, 1, 4, 0. What is the mean number of goals per game?

The mean is (1 + 0 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 0) / 8 = 12 / 8 = 1.5 goals per game.

Example: A restaurant manager records tip amounts from 5 tables: $5, $8, $7, $50, $6. Should the manager use the mean or median to report the typical tip, and why?

The $50 tip is an outlier that raises the mean to $15.20, which is far above most tips. The median of $7 is more representative of what a typical table leaves.

Example: A student earns the following scores on four exams weighted differently: Exam 1 (weight 10%) = 80, Exam 2 (weight 20%) = 90, Exam 3 (weight 30%) = 70, Exam 4 (weight 40%) = 85. What is the weighted mean?

Weighted mean = (80 x 0.10) + (90 x 0.20) + (70 x 0.30) + (85 x 0.40) = 8 + 18 + 21 + 34 = 81.0.

Example: A company surveyed 9 employees about their commute times (in minutes): 10, 15, 20, 20, 25, 25, 25, 30, 35. The CEO (with a 90-minute commute) is added. How does this change the mean and median?

Original mean is about 22.8 and median is 25. After adding 90, the mean jumps to about 29.5 while the median shifts to 25 (average of 25 and 25). The outlier affects the mean far more than the median.

summary_sections.title: Special Topics
summary_sections.content_html:
Important Concepts

A bimodal data set has:

The prefix 'bi-' means two, so bimodal describes a data set that has exactly two values that appear most frequently.

What is a weighted mean

A weighted mean assigns different importance (weights) to data values, computed as the sum of (value times weight) divided by the sum of all weights.

What does 'bimodal' mean in statistics

Bimodal means the data set has two distinct modes -- two values that each appear more frequently than any other values in the set.

What does 'skewed data' mean in relation to measures of center

Skewed data has a distribution where one tail is longer than the other. In right-skewed data the mean is greater than the median, and in left-skewed data the mean is less than the median.

What is the 'frequency' of a data value

Frequency is the count of how many times a particular value occurs in a data set. It is used to determine the mode.

A student earns the following scores on four exams weighted differently: Exam 1 (weight 10%) = 80, Exam 2 (weight 20%) = 90, Exam 3 (weight 30%) = 70, Exam 4 (weight 40%) = 85. What is the weighted mean

Weighted mean = (80 x 0.10) + (90 x 0.20) + (70 x 0.30) + (85 x 0.40) = 8 + 18 + 21 + 34 = 81.0.
